content.text: Editing Code
Editing code

is the core workflow where Claude helps you modify files, add features, fix bugs, and refactor code. Instead of typing every change manually, you describe what you want and Claude proposes edits that you review and approve before applying.
The Edit Workflow
Claude's code editing follows a
review-first pattern
by default:

1. You describe the change
2. Claude reads relevant files
3. Claude proposes specific edits
4. You review the diff
5. You approve or reject
Workflow 1: Make Simple Changes
For straightforward edits to a known file:
update user.ts to add the null check you suggested
Steps:

1. Tell Claude what to change
2. Claude reads the file and proposes edits
3. Review the diff shown in terminal
4. Type
yes
to approve or
no
to reject
Workflow 2: Edit With Context
Use
to reference specific files or line ranges so Claude knows exactly what to edit:

Explain the logic in @src/utils/auth.js, then update it to add error handling
update @src/components/Login.tsx#10-45 to add form validation
Tips:

- File paths can be relative or absolute
- Use
@filename#lines

for specific line ranges
- You can reference multiple files in one message
Workflow 3: Refactor Code
For updating old code to use modern patterns:

find deprecated API usage in our codebase
suggest how to refactor utils.js to use modern JavaScript features
refactor utils.js to use ES2024 features while maintaining the same behavior
run tests for the refactored code
Tips:

- Ask Claude to explain the benefits of the modern approach
- Request backward compatibility when needed
- Do refactoring in small, testable increments
Workflow 4: Fix Bugs Efficiently
When you encounter an error and need to find and fix its source:

I'm seeing an error when I run npm test
suggest a few ways to fix the @ts-ignore in user.ts
update user.ts to add the null check you suggested
Tips:

- Tell Claude the command to reproduce the issue and get a stack trace
- Mention steps to reproduce the error
- Let Claude know if the error is intermittent or consistent
Workflow 5: Plan Before Editing (Safe Mode)
For changes you want to review completely before touching disk, use
Plan Mode
to analyze before editing:
claude --permission-mode plan
Or toggle mid-session with
Shift+Tab
In Plan Mode:

- Claude reads files and proposes a plan
-
No edits are made until you approve

- You can review the plan in your text editor
- Edit the plan before applying changes
Workflow 6: Auto-Accept Edits (Fast Mode)
For changes you're confident about, skip the approval step and let Claude apply edits automatically:
/permissions
Then enable "Allow auto-edit mode".
Creator workflow:

1. Start in Plan Mode and refine the plan iteratively
2. Once you like the plan,
switch to auto-accept edits mode
3. Claude can usually 1-shot it
A good plan is really important!
— Boris Cherny, Claude Code creator
Workflow 7: Work with Tests
For adding tests for uncovered code:

find functions in NotificationsService.swift that are not covered by tests
add tests for the notification service
add test cases for edge conditions in the notification service
run the new tests and fix any failures
Key advantage:
Claude generates tests that
follow your project's existing patterns

. When asking for tests:
- Be specific about what behavior to verify
- Ask for edge cases you might have missed
- Claude analyzes code paths and suggests tests for error conditions, boundary values, unexpected inputs
Workflow 8: Documentation Edits
For adding or updating documentation:

find functions without proper JSDoc comments in the auth module
add JSDoc comments to the undocumented functions in auth.js
improve the generated documentation with more context and examples
check if the documentation follows our project standards
Tips:

- Specify the documentation style (JSDoc, docstrings, etc.)
- Ask for examples in the documentation
- Request documentation for public APIs, interfaces, complex logic
Key Editing Concepts
Concept
What It Means
Diff review
Claude shows changes before applying; you approve/reject
Inline diff
In VS Code, compare original vs changes side-by-side; accept/reject
Auto-edit mode
Skip approval prompts for commonly used commands
Plan Mode
Analyze before editing; no changes until you approve
Line ranges
Use
@filename#10-45
to edit specific lines
Best Practices for Editing
Practice
Why It Matters
Start with a plan
Use Plan Mode to refine before switching to auto-edit
Give Claude verification
Add a feedback loop: run tests, bash command, or test in browser
Use subagents for large changes
Delegate exploration so only findings come back
Keep context under 60%
Don't exceed context limits; use
/compact
to summarize
Run formatter after edits
Use PostToolUse hook: `"PostToolUse": [{"matcher": "WriteEdit", "hooks": [{"type": "command", "command": "bun run format
true"}]}]`
Save to CLAUDE.md
Document mistakes and style conventions so Claude improves over time
Common Mistakes When Editing
Mistake
Better Approach
Asking overly broad tasks like "fix all issues in entire repo"
Be hyper-specific; isolate to files or subsystems
Skipping verification
Always add tests or run app to verify changes work
Not using Plan Mode for big changes
Use Plan Mode for complex refactors before editing
Auto-editing without a plan
Refine plan iteratively, then switch to auto-edit
Accepting formatting inconsistencies
Add PostToolUse hook to format code automatically
Mental Model
Think of editing code in Claude Code as having a
pair programmer who writes code for you to review
. You describe the change, they propose edits, and you decide what gets merged. The key is that you're
always in control
— Claude never modifies files without your approval (unless you enable auto-edit mode).
Key Takeaways
Review-first by default
— Claude shows diffs before applying
Use
@file
references
to point Claude at exact files or line ranges
Plan Mode for big changes
— analyze before editing
Auto-edit for confident changes
— skip approval for simple edits
Add verification
— tests, bash commands, browser testing improve quality 2–3x
Format automatically
— use PostToolUse hook to clean up formatting
How It Works in Practice
Understanding the internals of editing code helps you predict its behavior and debug issues effectively.

When a request is processed, several layers of the system interact: the client library validates inputs and manages retries, the transport layer handles HTTP/2 connection multiplexing, and the API server routes to the appropriate model instance. Each layer introduces latency that you can measure and optimize.

For production deployments, understanding this pipeline helps you allocate timeout budgets appropriately. The network round-trip typically adds 50-100ms, while model processing time scales with output length. A response generating 1,000 tokens takes 2-4x longer than one generating 250 tokens for the same model.
Best Practices
Production experience with editing code has surfaced these practices that consistently improve reliability and performance.
Use async clients for concurrent workloads — Python's AsyncAnthropic and TypeScript's async/await patterns avoid blocking the event loop during API calls.
Cache system prompts with cache_control — stable system prompts hit the cache ~90% of the time after the first call, reducing both latency and cost significantly.
Stream responses for interactive use — streaming reduces time-to-first-token from seconds to milliseconds, dramatically improving perceived responsiveness.
Implement structured retry logic — use exponential backoff starting at 1 second, with jitter to prevent thundering herd problems under rate limiting.
Log structured metadata — capture model, input_tokens, output_tokens, latency, and request_id in every log entry for effective debugging and cost tracking.
Set explicit max_tokens — always specify max_tokens to bound cost and prevent unexpectedly long responses that consume your token budget.
Common Pitfalls
Awareness of these common failure modes prevents hours of debugging in production.
Not validating response structure — even when you request JSON, parse it with a try/catch. Models occasionally produce malformed output under high load or edge-case inputs.
Accumulating conversation history without truncation — after 10-20 turns, conversations can exceed context windows. Implement a sliding window or summarization strategy.
Mixing prompt concerns — put behavior instructions in system prompts, task-specific content in user turns. Mixing them reduces clarity and makes iteration harder.
Ignoring stop_reason — check if stop_reason is "max_tokens" rather than "end_turn". Truncated responses need handling differently than complete ones.
Creating new client instances per request — instantiate the Anthropic client once at module load time to benefit from connection pooling across requests.
Performance Benchmarks and Cost Analysis
Making informed architectural decisions requires concrete numbers. These benchmarks are representative of typical production workloads.
Claude Haiku 3.5: ~$0.80/MTok input, ~$4/MTok output. Best for high-volume, simple tasks like classification, extraction, and summarization.
Claude Sonnet 4: ~$3/MTok input, ~$15/MTok output. The best quality-to-cost ratio for most production use cases requiring reasoning.
Prompt cache hit rate: After the first call, 85-95% of requests hit the cache for stable prompts. Cache tokens cost 10% of normal input token price.
Message Batches API: 50% cost reduction for async workloads processed within 24 hours. Ideal for nightly data processing, evaluation runs, and bulk content generation.
Typical latency: 1-3s for Haiku, 3-10s for Sonnet, 10-30s for Opus. Streaming makes all models feel 2-5x faster from the user's perspective.
Security Considerations
Deploying editing code in production requires careful attention to security boundaries and trust models.
API key management — store keys in environment variables or secrets managers (AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault). Never commit to version control.
Prompt injection prevention — when embedding user input in prompts, use XML tags to clearly delimit user content from instructions, reducing injection attack surface.
Output sanitization — before rendering AI-generated content in web UIs, escape HTML to prevent XSS attacks from model-produced content.
Rate limiting user requests — implement per-user rate limits in addition to API limits to prevent cost abuse and ensure fair service distribution.
Summary

Editing Code is a foundational capability for building production-ready Claude integrations. By following the patterns in this guide — proper error handling, async clients, prompt caching, and structured monitoring — you can build reliable, cost-efficient applications that scale gracefully from prototype to production.

Summary

Editing Code is a foundational capability for building production-ready Claude integrations. By following the patterns in this guide — proper error handling, async clients, prompt caching, and structured monitoring — you can build reliable, cost-efficient applications that scale gracefully from prototype to production.
How to Frame an Edit Request

The quality of Claude's code edits is almost entirely determined by the quality of the edit request. A well-framed request includes four elements: the file being edited (pasted in full or as a relevant excerpt), the specific change goal stated precisely, the constraints that must not be violated, and the expected output format. Vague requests like "fix the bug in this function" produce guesses; precise requests like "the calculate_tax function returns incorrect values for amounts above 100000 because the bracket logic stops at the second condition — fix the conditional chain" produce targeted edits. The more context you provide about what correct behavior looks like, the smaller the search space Claude has to navigate. When editing requires understanding dependencies outside the target file, provide those files too — Claude cannot infer what an imported function does from its name alone.
State the target file: paste the full file or the relevant function — partial excerpts that omit surrounding context cause off-by-one errors in edits.
Describe the change goal precisely: "add a timeout parameter" is better than "improve the network call."
State constraints explicitly: "do not change the function signature," "maintain backward compatibility," "keep the test suite passing."
Specify the expected output format: full file replacement, unified diff, or inline suggestion with explanation.
Provide dependent files: if the function being edited calls functions from other modules, paste those modules or their relevant sections.
For bug fixes, include a failing test case or a concrete input/output pair that demonstrates the incorrect behavior.
Specifying the Output Format for Edits

Claude can produce code edits in several formats, and choosing the right one for your workflow saves time on integration. Full file replacement is the simplest: Claude outputs the entire modified file, which you paste over the original. This is appropriate for small files where the diff is more than 30% of the content. Unified diff format (the output of git diff) is preferable for large files where most content is unchanged — it shows only the changed lines with context, which is faster to review and apply with the patch command. Inline suggestion format asks Claude to quote the original line and show the replacement, formatted as "Change line X from ... to ..." — this is most useful in conversational workflows where you want to understand each change before applying it. Specify your preferred format explicitly in the prompt; Claude defaults to full file replacement when no format is requested.
Full file replacement: best for files under 200 lines or when more than 30% of the content changes.
Unified diff: best for large files with small changes; apply with: patch original.py < changes.patch
Inline suggestion: "replace line 47 from X to Y" — best for conversational review before applying changes.
Ask Claude to explain each change alongside the edit when reviewing unfamiliar codebases.
For automated pipelines, request JSON output: {"file": "...", "hunks": [...]} for programmatic application.
Never ask for a diff without specifying the context lines — Claude may produce a diff with too little context to apply correctly.
Staged Versus All-at-Once Edits

Large refactors should almost always be broken into smaller, sequenced edit requests rather than asking Claude to restructure an entire codebase in one shot. This is not a limitation of Claude's capability — it is a risk management practice. When you accept a 500-line all-at-once refactor without reviewing intermediate states, errors compound silently. By contrast, staged edits let you run tests after each step, verify that the behavior is preserved, and catch regressions before they accumulate. A useful staging strategy is to extract the refactor into phases: first rename identifiers, then restructure the call graph, then change signatures, then update tests. Each phase is a self-contained edit request with a passing test suite checkpoint. For automated refactoring at scale, tools like ast-grep, jscodeshift, or libcst handle mechanical transformations more reliably than LLM edits, reserving Claude for the judgment-intensive steps.
Break large refactors into phases: rename → restructure → signature changes → test updates, with passing tests between each phase.
Run the test suite after every staged edit — this is the fastest way to detect when an edit broke something.
Use version control checkpoints: commit after each passing stage so you can git bisect if later stages fail.
For mechanical changes (rename all instances of foo to bar), use sed, ast-grep, or your IDE's find-and-replace — they are faster and more reliable.
Reserve Claude for judgment-intensive edits: restructuring logic, improving readability, or adding error handling.
All-at-once edits are appropriate for small files (under 100 lines) or when the change is a single, well-defined operation.
Edit Request Anti-Patterns to Avoid

Certain patterns in edit requests reliably produce poor results. The most common is the context-free request: pasting a single function and asking Claude to "improve it" without specifying what improvement means. Claude will make changes that seem reasonable in isolation but may violate conventions established elsewhere in the codebase. A related anti-pattern is the missing language version: asking Claude to modernize Python 2 code without specifying that the target is Python 3.11 risks receiving code that is valid Python 3 but uses deprecated 3.6 patterns. Another frequent mistake is asking Claude to edit a function that calls external services without providing the API contracts for those services — the edit will be semantically wrong because Claude has to guess the return types. Finally, never paste credentials, customer data, or secrets in edit requests, even if they appear in test fixtures or configuration files.
Context-free requests: always specify what "improvement" means — performance, readability, error handling, or type safety.
Missing language version: specify Python 3.11, Node 20, TypeScript 5.x, etc. — Claude's defaults may not match your target.
Missing API contracts: for functions that call external services, provide the relevant interface definitions or SDK types.
Overly large context: pasting a 2000-line file when only 50 lines are relevant dilutes attention — excerpt the relevant section.
Skipping test file updates: always ask Claude to update tests alongside implementation edits when tests exist.
Never paste secrets or customer PII in edit requests, even if they appear in test configuration or fixture files.

Well-Structured Edit Request Template
=====================================

FILE: src/services/payment_processor.py (full file pasted below)
LANGUAGE: Python 3.11

CHANGE GOAL:
The `charge_card` function currently raises a generic Exception when the
Stripe API returns a card_declined error code. Modify it to raise a
specific CardDeclinedError (already defined in src/exceptions.py) with
the decline code and message from the Stripe response.

CONSTRAINTS:
- Do not change the function signature (parameters or return type)
- Do not change how other error types are handled
- Keep the existing logging calls (do not remove or reformat them)
- CardDeclinedError is imported from src.exceptions — do not redefine it

EXPECTED OUTPUT: Unified diff format showing only the changed lines.

ADDITIONAL CONTEXT:
CardDeclinedError signature (from src/exceptions.py):
  class CardDeclinedError(PaymentError):
      def __init__(self, code: str, message: str): ...

Stripe error response shape:
  stripe.error.CardError has attributes:
    .code: str (e.g., "card_declined", "insufficient_funds")
    .user_message: str (human-readable decline reason)

[paste full file below this line]
